insights.title: ASR Team – Phase 3 Time Close Skip Analysis
insights.summary: Analyzed 47 ASR calls from the week of June 16–20. Found near-universal skip of the Phase 3 two-slot time close, with reps jumping from discovery directly to pitch close. This pattern correlated with significantly lower booking conversion.
main_findings: 96% of reps skipped the Phase 3 two-slot time close entirely
Reps who completed Phase 3 converted at 34% vs 18% for those who skipped
Average call time was 2.3 minutes shorter on skipped calls — reps are rushing
Most common skip point: after prospect confirms interest, rep immediately pitches the calendar link
positive_patterns: Discovery questions (Phase 1) executed well across 82% of calls
Tone was warm and conversational — no aggressive closers identified
Product knowledge was solid; reps answered product questions confidently
customer_objections: I need to check my schedule first
Can you just send me the info?
I'm not sure I have time right now
What exactly are we meeting about?
winning_language: 'I want to make sure this is worth your time — I have two slots that work for most folks...'
'Before I pull up the calendar, which works better for you — mornings or afternoons?'
'Most of our members said the same thing before they saw the full picture — 20 minutes is all it takes.'
pain_points: Golfers feel they've already tried 'everything' to improve
Time commitment concerns around training programs
Skepticism about online/digital coaching vs in-person
buying_triggers: Mention of specific handicap improvement numbers
Peer social proof ('other golfers just like you')
Money-back guarantee mention
Limited slot / urgency framing
opportunities: Implement mandatory Phase 3 checkpoint in QA rubric
Create a two-slot time close script card for ASR reps
Add Phase 3 as a scored item in AI QA rubric immediately
recommendations: Update QA rubric to score Phase 3 as a required checkpoint (not optional)
Run targeted Phase 3 role-play session in next team training
Add time-close language to the Master Script Hub sidebar
Flag any call under 9 minutes for supervisor review

action_items.task: Update AI QA rubric to include Phase 3 scoring
Create Phase 3 two-slot time close script card
Schedule team role-play session

transcript_excerpts.label: Example of skipped Phase 3 (common pattern)
transcript_excerpts.text: Rep: 'Great, so you're interested in improving your short game?' Prospect: 'Yeah, definitely.' Rep: 'Perfect, let me go ahead and send you a calendar link for the call!' [Phase 3 skipped entirely]

transcript_excerpts.label: Example of Phase 3 done correctly
transcript_excerpts.text: Rep: 'Perfect. Before I pull this up — do mornings or afternoons tend to work better for you?' Prospect: 'Afternoons are easier.' Rep: 'Great. I've got Tuesday at 2pm or Thursday at 3pm — which one works?' Prospect: 'Thursday works.' [Booked.]

insights.title: Retention Team – Billing Surprise as #1 Cancellation Driver
insights.summary: Retention call analysis across June 8–18 reveals that billing surprise around the $29/month Scratch Club subscription is the top stated reason for cancellation. Approximately 48% of callers expressed some form of 'I didn't know I was going to be charged.' Save rate currently running at 43% vs the 60% target.
main_findings: 48% of cancellation calls cited billing surprise as primary reason
Save rate at 43% MTD vs 60% target — 17-point gap
Refund rate well above 12% goal
65% of retention calls had zero golf context established before pitching the save offer
Reps are leading with price defense rather than value re-anchoring
positive_patterns: Reps who led with empathy and golf context first had a 61% save rate
Scratch Club value proposition resonated when explained clearly with specifics
Tone was generally calm and non-confrontational
customer_objections: I didn't know I was being charged $29 a month
I thought this was a one-time thing
I haven't even used it
It's not worth it for me right now
I'm just not playing golf right now
winning_language: 'I completely understand — let me take a look at your account and see what makes the most sense for you.'
'A lot of members feel that way before they actually log in — can I show you what's actually in there?'
'What's your game like right now? Are you still getting out much?'
pain_points: Customers feel deceived by unclear billing communication at point of sale
Low perceived usage / value in first 30 days
No personal connection to the product content
buying_triggers: Specific feature they haven't tried yet
Pause option instead of cancel
Partial refund as goodwill gesture
Connecting product to their specific golf goal
opportunities: Implement a 'golf context first' protocol before any save pitch
Create a billing expectation reset script for reps
Explore pause/hold option to reduce hard cancellations
Work with fulfillment to improve Day 1 onboarding email clarity
recommendations: Add mandatory golf context question before any retention pitch
Script a billing reset empathy sequence for reps (not defensive, empathetic)
Propose a 'pause for 30 days' offer as a save tool
Flag billing surprise calls for upstream marketing/checkout team

action_items.task: Draft billing surprise empathy script for retention team
Propose pause feature to Jeff/Denver
Audit Day 1 onboarding email for billing clarity

transcript_excerpts.label: Most common billing surprise opening
transcript_excerpts.text: Customer: 'I want to cancel. I didn't know I was going to be charged $29.' Rep: 'I understand. The charge is for your Scratch Club membership which you signed up for...' [Defensive framing — customer disengages]

insights.title: Closer Team – Top 3 Objection Types and Call Position Timing
insights.summary: Closer call review for June identified the top three objection categories by frequency and call-position timing: cost/spend fatigue (appearing at 28+ minute mark), equipment resistance (mid-call, 15–20 min), and video/technology skepticism (early call, 8–12 min). Understanding timing allows reps to pre-empt rather than react.
main_findings: Cost/spend fatigue is #1 objection — 67% of non-converted calls included it
Equipment resistance peaks at 15–20 minute mark — typically after program reveal
Video/tech skepticism surfaces early (8–12 min) and if unhandled, kills the call
Reps who pre-empted cost objection before it arose converted at 2.1x rate
Only 22% of reps used any pre-emption language in June
positive_patterns: Reps with strong rapport-building in first 10 minutes had 40% higher close rates
Story-based selling (member transformation stories) outperformed feature-listing
Reps who acknowledged skepticism and leaned in rather than defending closed better
customer_objections: I've already spent so much on golf stuff that didn't work
I'm not sure I need new equipment on top of this
I'm not great with video calls / technology
Can I think about it?
My wife/partner needs to know about this first
winning_language: 'Before we get into the investment — a lot of our members told me the same thing. Let me show you why this is different.'
'The technology piece is honestly the easiest part — our team walks you through everything in the first session.'
'You don't need any new equipment. This works with exactly what you have in your bag right now.'
pain_points: Past purchase regret — golfers have wasted money on gear, lessons, apps before
Tech anxiety — older demographic uncomfortable with video coaching
Spouse approval required — purchase decisions not made independently
buying_triggers: Specific transformation story matching their handicap range
No-equipment-needed confirmation
Guarantee / risk reversal language
Coach expertise and credibility mention
Limited enrollment / cohort framing
opportunities: Build objection pre-emption scripts for each of the top 3 objections
Create a 'tech onboarding assurance' talking point for video-skeptical prospects
Develop spouse/partner inclusion script for end-of-call scenarios
Train reps to identify which objection type is dominant in first 10 minutes
recommendations: Add objection pre-emption module to closer onboarding and monthly training
Create a 'Top 3 Objections Cheat Sheet' with timing guidance for each
Score pre-emption language in QA rubric for closer calls
Analyze non-converted calls specifically for unhandled tech skepticism

action_items.task: Build Top 3 Objections pre-emption script card
Add pre-emption scoring to closer QA rubric
Present findings to closer team in weekly meeting

transcript_excerpts.label: Cost objection handled with pre-emption (winning example)
transcript_excerpts.text: Rep: 'Before I walk you through what this looks like investment-wise — I want to acknowledge something. A lot of the guys I talk to have spent money on things that didn't deliver. So I want to be upfront about why this is structured differently.' [Prospect engagement increases — less defensive when cost revealed]

insights.title: Internal Setter Team – June 24 Full-Day Transcript Analysis (907 Calls)
insights.summary: Full analysis of 907 outbound Internal Setter calls placed on June 24, 2026. Of 907 total calls, 796 (87.8%) were unreachable or voicemail. Of the 111 live contacts, 27 resulted in a booked appointment (24.3% live-contact conversion), 30 declined, and 10 had follow-up potential. The top 3 decline drivers were cost, time/schedule conflict, and location limitations. Price objections surfaced as early as the opening minute on several calls. Reps who completed full discovery before revealing price averaged 10.9-minute calls and converted; those who answered cost questions immediately averaged 4.1-minute calls and lost the lead. Five Scratch Club cancellation calls were handled with zero save attempts.
main_findings: 907 total calls; 796 (87.8%) unreachable or VM — only 111 live contacts made
27 booked out of 111 live contacts = 24.3% live-contact conversion rate
Booked calls averaged 10.9 minutes vs 4.1 minutes for declined — a 2.6x talk-time gap that directly tracks with conversion
Top 3 decline types: Declined-Cost (6 calls), Declined-Time (6 calls), Declined-Location (4 calls)
Price objections appeared in the first 60 seconds on 3 of 4 cost-decline calls — before any discovery was run
Location objections were hard-stop exits — zero reps successfully converted a location objection in this dataset
Julius Vizcayno led all reps in declines (7) with only 2 bookings; Heinrich Abarquez had 5 declines and 1 booking — both need objection coaching
Dan Emmanuel Nicolas led the team in bookings (7) with only 3 declines — the highest conversion efficiency on the team
5 Scratch Club cancellation calls processed — zero retention attempts made on any of them
Canadian and international leads received the same pitch as domestic leads despite location and currency barriers making them unlikely to convert
positive_patterns: Reps who deflected the price question and ran full discovery before revealing cost had significantly longer calls and higher conversions
Phoebe Collado's booked call (Jane Dunwoodie, TMA Booked) demonstrated the full script structure correctly: warm open, full discovery, root-flaw framing, time close to Saturday Zoom
Dan Emmanuel Nicolas showed consistent booking efficiency — the highest booked-to-declined ratio on the team (7 bookings / 3 declines)
Social proof framing with 9,000 golfers and top-10-coach credibility was deployed well on booked calls
Root-flaw language ('we find the root cause underneath it all') was the strongest differentiator when used — it visibly shifted prospect engagement
customer_objections: "I was really just fishing to see what the price point is." — Richard Ian Alvarez call, stated in opening
"I'd rather just cut to the chase — if it's a price point I can't afford, there's no sense going through my game." — Glenn Huth
"Wow, okay, yeah." — after hearing $3,000–$4,000, call declined within seconds
"I'm not going to Florida or Texas or California to take a golf lesson. I want something right here in St. Louis, Missouri." — Cathy York
"You're not answering the question. Where — what's closest to Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania?" — Jim Steigerwaldt
"I can't take 5 days and go to Florida to a golf academy. I'm not in a position to do that because of demands in my profession." — C. Randolph Keller
"I have too many things going on in my life. My son's getting married, I've been going back and forth to Delaware, I've been working a lot of overtime." — Thomas McGowan
"I don't play enough golf. I thought if it was a video or something I'd watch it, but I don't play enough." — Rod Lang
"I didn't even know that I was a member. I'd like to cancel my membership. You guys keep taking money out of my account automatically." — Ho yean Song (Scratch Club)
"I'm getting a bill every month, so I would like to cancel the one I'm getting charged for." — Louise Finch (Scratch Club)
"It was like $30 a month, and I retired, so you know what I mean." — Bryan Kugler (Scratch Club)
winning_language: "We've worked with over 9,000 golfers. One thing that separates us from local lessons or YouTube is we don't just fix the miss — we find the root cause, the one thing underneath it all that keeps pulling everything else out." (Phoebe Collado — TMA booked)

"There's really two kinds of golfers: guys who enjoy the game, and guys who, if they're honest, know they're capable of playing better and are actually ready to do something about it. Which one are you?" (Phoebe Collado — strong identity trigger)

"Since everything is customized, same goes with the cost — cost varies from person to person. The reason I called is to make sure the program is a good fit for you first." (price deflection that enables discovery)
"What's your goal from a score standpoint within a year?" (score-goal anchor — used well on all booked calls)
"Most members know what their miss is — they just don't know why it keeps coming back. That's what we solve." (root-flaw positioning)
pain_points: Chipping and short game — most cited pain point across both booked and declined calls: deceleration, inconsistency around the green, pulling chips left
Driver inconsistency — second most common: slice, push-fade, direction control
Time poverty — many leads have genuine interest but work schedules, family obligations, and caregiving are real blockers (not soft objections)
Location friction — U.S.-only academy footprint is a hard ceiling for Canadian prospects and golfers in states with no nearby location (Nebraska, Missouri, western Pennsylvania)
Price shock — the $3,000–$4,000 price point landing cold, without value framing or discovery, triggers immediate exit
Low play frequency — several leads self-disqualified because they only play once or twice a month and feel the academy investment doesn't fit their commitment level
Scratch Club billing confusion — recurring subscription charges not remembered or understood by multiple customers; mirrors pattern identified in insight-002
buying_triggers: Root cause framing — 'we find the root flaw, not just the miss' was the strongest engagement trigger on booked calls
Customization language — 'everything is built around your specific game' reduced price resistance before the number was revealed
Coach credibility — 'top 10, top 20 coaches in the US' created perceived value that justified the investment
Score goal anchoring — getting the prospect to name their 1-year goal made them emotionally invested before the pitch
Social proof at scale — '9,000 golfers' created safety and credibility
Identity question — 'which one are you?' triggered self-selection as a serious golfer and created personal ownership of the next step
Florida location density — 'we have 12–13 academies in Florida alone' reassured location-concerned prospects in the Southeast
opportunities: PRICE PRE-EMPTION SCRIPT: Build a confident redirect for when prospects ask about cost before discovery is complete — this is happening in the first 60 seconds on roughly 30% of connected calls and needs a scripted response, not ad-hoc handling
LOCATION QUALIFIER AT OPEN: Add a state/location screen to the first 2 minutes of the script — reps are running 4–6 minute full pitches only to hit a hard location DQ at the end
SCRATCH CLUB SAVE PROTOCOL: Zero retention attempts were made on any of the 5 cancellation calls processed by the setter team — this is a direct monthly revenue leak that needs an immediate fix

DOWNSELL OFFER FOR DQ'D LEADS: For prospects who self-DQ on price or play frequency, there is currently no fallback offer — a PG1 digital program or online coaching option would capture partial revenue from these exits

TARGETED COACHING — JULIUS AND HEINRICH: Combined 12 declines vs 3 bookings in this session; focused coaching on objection pre-emption and discovery sequencing would have a measurable impact on team conversion

CANADIAN/INTERNATIONAL LEAD FLOW: Canadian prospects are receiving the same full pitch as domestic leads before anyone confirms they can travel — a modified short flow with upfront logistics framing would reduce wasted call time
recommendations: Add a Price Pre-Emption Block to the setter script: when a prospect asks about cost before discovery, the scripted response should be 'Great question — and I want to make sure the number I give you is actually relevant to your specific game. Give me 3 minutes, and I'll be able to tell you exactly what this looks like for you.'
Add a location qualifier in the first 2 minutes: 'Just so I can point you to the closest location — what state are you in?' This one question prevents 4+ minute calls that end in a hard DQ
Implement a Scratch Club save script for setter reps handling inbound cancellations — at minimum: value re-anchor, usage question, and pause offer before processing the cancel
Introduce a downsell path (PG1 digital / online coaching) for leads who cite price or low play frequency — currently these calls end in zero revenue when they don't have to
Schedule individual coaching sessions for Julius Vizcayno and Heinrich Abarquez focused on discovery-first sequencing and objection pre-emption before cost reveal
Work with Lhen to flag Canadian and international CRM leads for a modified pitch flow that addresses USD pricing and travel requirements upfront

action_items.task: Write Price Pre-Emption Block for setter script (cost question redirect)
Add state/location qualifier to setter script Phase 1 opening
Build Scratch Club save micro-script for setter cancellation calls
Design downsell flow (PG1 digital) for price/frequency DQ'd leads
Schedule 1:1 coaching sessions with Julius Vizcayno and Heinrich Abarquez
Flag Canadian and international leads in CRM for modified pitch flow

transcript_excerpts.label: OBJECTION #1 — Price before discovery, call lost immediately (Heinrich Abarquez / Glenn Huth)
transcript_excerpts.text:
Contact: "Yeah, honestly, I'd rather just cut to the chase because if it's a price point I can't afford, there's no sense going through my game and all that kind of thing."

Contact: "$3,000 before I would do anything with food or travel or anything like that then is what you're saying, right? $4,000."

Contact: "Wow, okay, yeah." [Call declined within seconds]

transcript_excerpts.label: OBJECTION #2 — Location hard-stop, no workaround (Heinrich Abarquez / Cathy York)
transcript_excerpts.text:
Contact: "Yeah, but I live in St. Louis, Missouri, and so if there isn't anything here, I'm not interested."

Agent: "Okay, sure, I understand that Missouri is your focus right now, but we have locations all ac—"

Contact: "I'm not going to Florida or Texas or California to take a golf lesson. I want something right here in St. Louis, Missouri."

transcript_excerpts.label: OBJECTION #3 — Time and life conflict blocking schedule commitment (Vian Perez / C. Randolph Keller)
transcript_excerpts.text:
Contact: "I can't take 5 days and go to Florida to a golf academy per se. I'm not in a position to do that just because of demands in my profession and work."

Contact: "I'm very, very busy. And that's why I wanted to hear a little bit about what the game improvement options were."

transcript_excerpts.label: WINNING PATTERN — Discovery-first pivot, full script, booked (Phoebe Collado / Jane Dunwoodie)
transcript_excerpts.text:
Agent: "Since everything is customized, same goes with the cost — cost varies from person to person. The reason I called is I want to make sure the program is a good fit for you first. Would you mind giving me a quick picture of your game?"

[Full discovery: chipping pain point identified, root-flaw framed, score goal anchored at 'below 50 for 9 holes', 'which one are you?' identity close used, Saturday 2 PM Zoom booked]

Outcome: E. TMA Booked

transcript_excerpts.label: SCRATCH CLUB BILLING SURPRISE — No save attempted (Richard Ian Alvarez / Ho yean Song)
transcript_excerpts.text:
Contact: "The reason I'm calling you to cancel my membership — I didn't even know that I was a member, and I realized it a few days ago. I'd like to cancel my membership. Is it possible? Because you guys keep taking the money out of my account automatically."

Agent: "Yeah, no problem, sir. We can cancel this. I'll go ahead and do that for you."

[Cancel processed immediately. No retention attempt. No value re-anchor. No pause offer. Zero revenue recovered.]

insights.title: ASR Team Lee – June 24 Post-Purchase PG-One Onboarding Call Analysis
insights.product: PG-One System / SF-One Driver / RS-ONE Putter / 357 Fairway Wood / Simple Strike Sequence / One-Shot Slice Fix
insights.team_member: Marwin Ray Reyes, Emmanuella Frago, Gabriella Abem, Carlo Palma, Kim Paolo Planas
insights.summary: Full analysis of ASR Team Lee outbound post-purchase follow-up calls placed on June 24, 2026. All live contacts resulted in a booked PG-One advisor appointment — a 100% live-contact booking rate. Calls covered five reps, multiple products (SF-One Driver, RS-ONE Putter, 357 Fairway Wood Hybrid, Simple Strike Sequence Pro, One-Shot Slice Fix, PG-One Premium), and both outbound and one inbound callback. The universal pattern across every call: zero discovery questions asked, no VIP coaching offers made, and transactional (rather than consultative) delivery. Average QA scores ranged from 6/10 (Carlo Palma — service calls) to 8.5/10 (Gabriella Abem). The team excels at scheduling efficiency but leaves significant value on the table by skipping discovery and enthusiasm-building on every call.
main_findings: 100% live-contact booking rate — every connected call resulted in a confirmed PG-One advisor appointment
Zero discovery questions asked across all calls — the 4-question framework (game, shots, short game, score goal) was skipped universally
No VIP coaching offers made on any call — a missed upsell opportunity on every interaction
Gabriella Abem scored highest (8.5/10) — cleanest execution, best scheduling efficiency, strongest appointment detail confirmation
Carlo Palma scored lowest (6–6.5/10) across multiple calls — needs tonality, enthusiasm, and discovery coaching
Emmanuella Frago scored consistently in the 7–7.5/10 range — strong persistence on scheduling objections but weak on discovery and golf knowledge
Marwin Ray Reyes scored 8/10 — best rapport and humor of the team; strongest call personality
One inbound callback (Brett Maida / Emmanuella) was handled correctly with a clean 'is this required?' objection reframe
Two calls (Carlo Palma / Bruce Larson and Gabriella Abem / Curt Bousum) successfully resolved account issues (wrong email/address, checkout billing confusion) while still booking the appointment
Login/access issues were identified on at least 2 calls — a signal that onboarding email clarity or app UX may need attention
positive_patterns: Marwin Ray Reyes — Best rapport of the team: warm humor ('feels like Monday'), natural conversational cadence, precise appointment details (advisor name, Fort Lauderdale, time zone). Score: 8/10
Gabriella Abem (Tracy Clites call) — Highest-scoring call (8.5/10): efficient, professional, no objections, all scheduling details confirmed cleanly including time zone and advisor name

Gabriella Abem (Curt Bousum call) — Excellent real-time order verification: immediately confirmed only SF-One was charged, addressed billing concern without defensiveness, committed to shipping follow-up
Emmanuella Frago (Brett Maida inbound) — Smooth handling of 'is this required?' objection: reframed onboarding as value-add rather than obligation, confirmed Eastern time zone proactively
Carlo Palma — Professional and courteous throughout all calls; non-pushy positioning of advisor call as optional support rather than sales pressure preserved customer goodwill
Universal strength: appointment detail precision — all reps consistently confirmed date, time, time zone, advisor name, and Fort Lauderdale origin across every booking
customer_objections: "I'm at work right now... we're booked up and busy... I've been up since midnight." — Charles Walker (scheduling constraint — resolved with 5:20 PM ET next day)
"Is this like a requirement for purchasing the club?" — Brett Maida (obligation concern — reframed as value-add)

"I'm not interested in that [357 EZ Fairway promotion at $249], so I didn't complete it. I just wanted to make sure you didn't have that on my order." — Curt Bousum (checkout billing concern — resolved in real-time)
"I did purchase it, but I couldn't get my login." — Christina Haven (technical access issue — advisor callback scheduled for Friday)
"I haven't downloaded it [the app] yet." — Harold Reece, Don Doroha (inaction — handled by scheduling advisor check-in without pressure)
"Tomorrow isn't a good time [due to golf tournaments]." — (scheduling conflict — pivoted to Friday without resistance)
winning_language: "Included with your [product], you also have access to our PG-One system — it's a personalized game improvement system that's going to have you working on the right things in the right order instead of guessing, so you start seeing results faster." (universal PG-One value intro — used consistently across all reps)

"She's one of our best. She's an expert. So I suggest you ask a lot of things about the app, a lot of tips, suggestions, whatnot." (Marwin Ray Reyes — advisor credibility language that builds anticipation)

"Our really main goal here is for you to fully understand how your PG-One works and how it could really help you out with your golf game." (Emmanuella Frago — customer-centric reframe that diffused resistance)

"I can ask one of our PG-One advisors to give you a call back and help you log in, make sure the system is customized for you." (Marwin Ray Reyes / Christina Haven — service-first language that converts login frustration into a booked appointment)

"Just in case your availability changes, you already have my number — just send me a text, call me back, or voicemail. Either way, we can reschedule it for you." (rescheduling safety net — used universally, reduces no-show risk)
pain_points: Login/access barriers — at least 2 customers had not downloaded the app or had login difficulties; onboarding email may not be driving activation effectively
Busy schedules — most common scheduling objection across all calls; customers citing work demands, church, golf tournaments, and variable shift times as barriers
Checkout confusion — at least 1 customer (Curt Bousum) was confused by a promotional upsell appearing during checkout and concerned about being double-charged
Generic pitch — customers hear the PG-One value proposition with no connection to their specific game, reducing perceived relevance
Low app engagement — several customers had not watched training videos or explored the app before the call; no pre-call activation strategy in place
buying_triggers: Advisor credibility framing — naming the advisor ('she's one of our best'), mentioning Fort Lauderdale origin, and describing the call as a personalized walkthrough increased appointment commitment
Flexibility in scheduling — offering morning/afternoon/evening and multi-day options was the key unlock for scheduling-objection customers
Service framing — positioning the advisor call as 'help' rather than a sales call reduced resistance on every call
Rescheduling safety net — 'if anything changes, just text me' consistently landed well and closed commitments that might otherwise stay soft
opportunities: DISCOVERY BEFORE SCHEDULING: The 4-question discovery framework (game, shots, short game, score goal) was skipped on every single call — adding even 1–2 questions before scheduling would personalize the advisor call and increase show-up rates
VIP COACHING OFFER: No rep offered VIP coaching on any call — post-purchase follow-up calls are warm, low-resistance moments that are ideal for introducing premium coaching options

GOLF KNOWLEDGE & CREDIBILITY: Reps are using generic language ('right things in the right order') but cannot demonstrate product-specific golf knowledge — connecting RS-ONE Putter to putting consistency or SF-One Driver to distance/forgiveness would build credibility
ENTHUSIASM & TONALITY: Multiple calls were described as 'transactional,' 'flat,' or 'monotone' — energy and excitement in the close would increase appointment show-up rates

APP ACTIVATION LANGUAGE: No rep encouraged customers to watch a specific training video or explore one feature before the advisor call — a pre-call activation prompt would increase engagement and show-up rates
ONBOARDING EMAIL REVIEW: Login issues on 2+ calls suggest the onboarding email may not be effectively driving app activation — worth auditing delivery, clarity, and CTA
recommendations: Add 1–2 lightweight discovery questions before scheduling: 'Before I book you with our advisor — what's the biggest challenge you're working on in your game right now?' This personalizes the advisor call and increases perceived value

Add a VIP coaching touchpoint to the post-purchase script: after booking the advisor appointment, introduce the VIP coaching option as a natural next step — 'Beyond the PG-One walkthrough, we also have VIP one-on-one coaching — want me to have someone share more info?'

Create product-specific credibility language for each club/product: one sentence per product that connects it to a real golf improvement outcome (e.g., 'The RS-ONE Putter is engineered for green consistency — that's where most golfers drop strokes')

Add a pre-call activation prompt at close: 'Before your call Friday, try logging into the app and watching the first training video — that way [Advisor Name] can pick up right where you are.' This increases engagement and show-up rate
Schedule Carlo Palma for targeted tonality and discovery coaching — his calls averaged 6–6.5/10 with consistent feedback on flat delivery, missing enthusiasm, and no discovery questions
Audit the post-purchase onboarding email for login clarity and activation CTA — login issues on multiple calls suggest it is not driving sufficient app engagement before the advisor call

action_items.task: Add 1–2 discovery questions to ASR post-purchase onboarding script
Add VIP coaching touchpoint to post-purchase script close
Create product-specific credibility language (one line per product)
Schedule targeted coaching session with Carlo Palma (tonality + discovery)
Audit post-purchase onboarding email for login clarity and app activation CTA

transcript_excerpts.label: BEST CALL — Warm rapport, precise close (Marwin Ray Reyes / Steve Cedoz)
transcript_excerpts.text:
Agent: 'Alright, so we will have your PG-One onboarding. That's going to be tomorrow at noon, 12 PM Eastern. Her name is [Advisor], by the way — she's one of our best. She's an expert. So I suggest you ask a lot of things about the app, tips, suggestions, whatnot.'

Contact: 'Okay, sounds great. I appreciate the call.'

[Appointment booked, advisor named and credentialed, rescheduling safety net provided. Rubric: Met. Score: 8/10]

transcript_excerpts.label: SCHEDULING OBJECTION HANDLED — Persistence without pressure (Emmanuella Frago / Charles Walker)
transcript_excerpts.text:
Contact: 'I can't schedule anything like that — we could be here till 12, 1 o'clock, or 4 or 5 this evening. We just never know.'

Agent: 'I hear you, sir. I do have schedules for even after 5 PM if you're already home by then.'

Contact: 'Well, I don't know — if I get home by 5 I'll be getting ready for church.'

Agent: 'Do you think you can carve out 15 minutes with our advisor?'

Contact: 'If they want to try tomorrow, that'd be fine — after 5.'

[Appointment booked: 5:20 PM ET next day. Score: 7/10 — persistence noted, but discovery questions skipped and same scheduling offers repeated rather than reframing value]

transcript_excerpts.label: BILLING CONCERN RESOLVED IN REAL-TIME (Gabriella Abem / Curt Bousum)
transcript_excerpts.text:
Contact: 'I wasn't sure — I wanted to double-check that you didn't have the 357 on my order. I'm not interested in that.'

Agent: 'I got you. I'm looking at your profile right now, and I do see that it was only the SF-One that was purchased.'

[Real-time order verification. Address confirmed. Shipping follow-up committed. Appointment booked: 9:40 AM CT. Score: 7.5/10 — excellent objection handling, but no discovery or golf knowledge deployed]

transcript_excerpts.label: FLAT DELIVERY, NO DISCOVERY (Carlo Palma / Harold Reece — lowest scoring call)
transcript_excerpts.text:
Agent: 'We just want to let you know that you now have access to our PG-One, which is our game improvement system. We just wanted to check back whether you have been able to download the app and log into it and watch the free training videos.'

Contact: 'No, I haven't downloaded it.'

Agent: 'Oh, okay, got it. Well, we just want to let you know we sent the login credentials to your email. If you have difficulties, I can let our PG-One advisor double-check on you probably on Friday.'

[Appointment booked: Friday 4 PM. Score: 6/10 — professional but transactional. Zero discovery, zero enthusiasm, zero VIP offer. Coaching needed on tonality and discovery sequencing]
